title: IICRC S500 Equipment Calculator — How RestoreAssist Calculates Dehumidifiers, Air Movers & Scrubbers
description: See how RestoreAssist uses IICRC S500 ratios to automatically calculate the correct number of dehumidifiers, air movers, and air scrubbers for any water damage job — and how it performs the AS/NZS 3012 electrical load check.
transcript: The IICRC S500 Equipment Calculator in RestoreAssist automates the calculation of drying equipment based on affected area and damage class. Input your square metres and damage class, and the system returns the minimum number of dehumidifiers at 1 per 40m2, air movers at 1 per 15m2, and air scrubbers at 1 per 100m2 — all cited against IICRC S500 ratios. It also performs an AS/NZS 3012:2019 electrical load check to ensure your equipment placement stays within 80% of circuit capacity.
videoScript:
G'day — today I'm going to show you the equipment calculator inside RestoreAssist, and how it uses IICRC S500 ratios to take the guesswork out of drying equipment placement.

If you've ever stood on a job wondering whether you've got enough dehumidifiers, or tried to calculate air mover counts for a large commercial loss on the back of a clipboard, this feature is going to save you significant time — and protect you when insurers audit your equipment justification.

Let's start with the fundamentals. IICRC S500:2025 defines minimum equipment ratios based on damage class and affected area. These ratios form the baseline for defensible scope documentation. The standard doesn't leave it to gut feel — it gives you specific numbers, and RestoreAssist applies them automatically.

Here's how the calculator works. Navigate to any inspection, open the Equipment Calculator tab, and enter three things: the total affected area in square metres, the damage class — one through four — and the water category.

The system calculates dehumidifiers at a ratio of one unit per 40 square metres of affected floor area for Class 2 and 3 losses. For Class 4 specialty drying, the ratio adjusts to account for deeper structural penetration. These ratios are pulled directly from IICRC S500 Table 6-2 and are displayed next to each calculated quantity so you have a clear audit trail.

Air movers are calculated at one unit per 15 square metres of affected floor area. This ensures adequate airflow across the drying surface to promote evaporation. Again, the IICRC reference is cited in the output so your scope documentation is self-explanatory.

For jobs where the water category is 2 or 3, the calculator also adds air scrubbers — one per 100 square metres — to address airborne contaminants. These are created as ScopeItems marked as auto-determined, so you can see exactly which line items were generated by the calculator versus manually added.

Now here's a feature that's particularly important for Australian sites: the AS/NZS 3012:2019 electrical load check. Australian workplace health and safety legislation requires that portable electrical equipment on construction sites — and restoration sites are treated the same way — must not load a circuit beyond 80% of its rated capacity.

RestoreAssist runs this check automatically based on the amperage draw of your equipment and the circuit capacity you specify for the site. If your calculated equipment load exceeds 80%, the system flags it with a warning and suggests splitting across multiple circuits. This is a real compliance gap we see on jobs — technicians place the equipment correctly per IICRC ratios, but inadvertently overload a single circuit.

All of this — the equipment quantities, the IICRC ratio justifications, and the electrical load check result — exports directly into your scope narrative and your insurance report. When an assessor reviews your claim, every equipment line item has a cited standard and a calculated basis. That's the difference between a smooth approval and a drawn-out request for justification.

If you'd like to see the equipment calculator in action on your jobs, visit restoreassist.com.au. The calculator is available on the Professional plan and above, and there's a 14-day free trial so you can test it on a real job before committing. I'll see you in the next one.
